title: Sci-Fi Writer Arthur C. Clarke Predicts the Future in 1964: Artificial Intelligence, Instantaneous Global Communication, Remote Work, Singularity & More
description:
in Science, Technology |   	April 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Are you feel┬нing con┬нfi┬нdent about the future? No? We under┬нstand. Would you
body:
in Science, Technology |   	April 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Are you feel┬нing con┬нfi┬нdent about the future? No? We under┬нstand. Would you like to know what it was like to feel a deep cer┬нtain┬нty that the decades to come were going to be filled with won┬нder and the fan┬нtas┬нtic? Well then, gaze upon this clip from the BBC Archive YouTube chan┬нnel of sci-fi author Arthur C. Clarke pre┬нdict┬нing the future in 1964.
Although we best know him for writ┬нing 2001: A Space Odyssey, the 1964 tele┬нvi┬нsion-view┬нing pub┬нlic would have known him for his futur┬нism and his tal┬нent for calm┬нly explain┬нing all the great things to come. In the late 1940s, he had already pre┬нdict┬нed telecom┬нmu┬нni┬нca┬нtion satel┬нlites. In 1962 he pub┬нlished his col┬нlect┬нed essays, Pro┬нfiles of the Future, which con┬нtains many of the ideas in this clip.
Here he cor┬нrect┬нly pre┬нdicts the ease with which we can be con┬нtact┬нed wher┬нev┬нer in the world we choose to, where we can con┬нtact our friends тАЬany┬нwhere on earth even if we donтАЩt know their loca┬нtion.тАЭ What Clarke doesnтАЩt pre┬нdict here is how тАЬloca┬нtionтАЭ isnтАЩt a thing when weтАЩre on the inter┬нnet. He imag┬нines peo┬нple work┬нing just as well from Tahi┬нti or Bali as they do from Lon┬нdon. Clarke sees this advance┬нment as the down┬нfall of the mod┬нern city, as we do not need to com┬нmute into the city to work. Now, as so many of us are doing our jobs from home post-COVID, weтАЩve also dis┬нcov┬нered the dystopia in that fan┬нta┬нsy. (It cer┬нtain┬нly has┬нnтАЩt dropped the cost of rent.)
Next, he pre┬нdicts advances in biotech┬нnol┬нo┬нgy that would allow us to, say, train mon┬нkeys to work as ser┬нvants and work┬нers. (Until, he jokes, they form a union and тАЬweтАЩd be back right where we start┬нed.) Per┬нhaps, he says, humans have stopped evolvingтАФwhat comes next is arti┬нfi┬нcial intel┬нli┬нgence (although that phrase had yet to be used) and machine evo┬нlu┬нtion, where weтАЩd be hon┬нored to be the тАЬstep┬нping stoneтАЭ towards that des┬нtiny. Make of that what you will. I know you might think it would be cool to have a mon┬нkey but┬нler, but cтАЩmon, think of the ethics, not to men┬нtion the cost of bananas.
Point┬нing out where Clarke gets it wrong is too easyтАФnobody gets it right all of the time. How┬нev┬нer, it is fas┬нci┬нnat┬нing that some things that have nev┬нer come to passтАФbeing able to learn a lan┬нguage overnight, or eras┬нing your memoriesтАФhave man┬нaged to resur┬нface over the years as sci┬нence fic┬нtion films, like Eter┬нnal Sun┬нshine of the Spot┬нless Mind. His ideas of cryo┬нgenic sus┬нpen┬нsion are sta┬нples of numer┬нous hard sci-fi films.
And we are still wait┬нing for the тАЬRepli┬нca┬нtorтАЭ machine, which would make exact dupli┬нcates of objects (and by so doing cause a col┬нlapse into тАЬglut┬нto┬нnous bar┬нbarismтАЭ because weтАЩd want unlim┬нit┬нed amounts of every┬нthing.) Some com┬нmenters call this a pre┬нcur┬нsor to 3тАСD print┬нing. IтАЩd say oth┬нer┬нwise, but some┬нthing very close to it might be around the cor┬нner. Who knows? Clarke him┬нself agrees about all this conjectureтАФitтАЩs doomed to fail.
тАЬThat is why the future is so end┬нless┬нly fas┬нci┬нnat┬нing. Try as we can, weтАЩll nev┬нer out┬нguess it.тАЭ
Note: An ear┬нli┬нer ver┬нsion of this post appeared on our site in 2022.
Relat┬нed Con┬нtent:
How French Artists in 1899 Envi┬нsioned What Life Would Look Like in the Year 2000┬а

Ted Mills is a free┬нlance writer on the arts.

title: Isaac Asimov Reviews George OrwellтАЩs Nineteen Eighty-Four and Calls It тАЬNot Science Fiction, But a Distorted Nostalgia for a Past that Never WasтАЭ
description: Here in the twen┬нty-twen┬нties,┬аa young read┬нer first hear┬нing of George OrwellтАЩs Nine┬нteen Eighty-Four would hard┬нly imag┬нine it to be a work of
body:
Here in the twen┬нty-twen┬нties,┬аa young read┬нer first hear┬нing of George OrwellтАЩs Nine┬нteen Eighty-Four would hard┬нly imag┬нine it to be a work of sci┬нence fic┬нtion. That would┬нnтАЩt have been the case in 1949, when the nov┬нel was first pub┬нlished, and when the epony┬нmous year would have sound┬нed like the dis┬нtant future. Even as the actu┬нal nine┬нteen-eight┬нies came around, it still evoked visions of a tech┬нno-total┬нi┬нtar┬нi┬нan dystopia ahead. тАЬSo thor┬нough┬нly has 1984-opho┬нbia pen┬нe┬нtrat┬нed the con┬нscious┬нness of many who have not read the book and have no notion of what it con┬нtains, that one won┬нders what will hap┬нpen to us after 31 Decem┬нber 1984,тАЭ wrote Isaac Asi┬нmov in 1980. тАЬWhen New YearтАЩs Day of 1985 arrives and the Unit┬нed States is still in exis┬нtence and fac┬нing very much the prob┬нlems it faces today, how will we express our fears of what┬нev┬нer aspect of life fills us with appre┬нhen┬нsion?тАЭ
The occa┬нsion was one of a series of syn┬нdi┬нcat┬нed news┬нpa┬нper columns that Asi┬нmov seems to have pub┬нlished each new year. At the dawn of Nine┬нteen Eighty-FourтАЩs decade, the syn┬нdi┬нcate asked him to revis┬нit OrwellтАЩs nov┬нel, which had already been a com┬нmon cul┬нtur┬нal ref┬нer┬нence for decades. As a work of sci┬нence fic┬нtion (the genre for which his own name had prac┬нti┬нcal┬нly come to stand), he finds it lack┬нing, to say the least. тАЬThe Lon┬нdon in which the sto┬нry is placed is not so much moved thir┬нty-five years for┬нward in time, from 1949 to 1984, as it is moved a thou┬нsand miles east in space to Moscow,тАЭ he writes. Far from attempt┬нing to imag┬нine the future, in Asi┬нmovтАЩs view, Orwell sim┬нply con┬нvert┬нed the Eng┬нland he knew into a drea┬нry Stal┬нin┬нist-type state. Apart from cer┬нtain implau┬нsi┬нble sur┬нveil┬нlance sys┬нtems, the set┬нting is тАЬincred┬нi┬нbly old-fash┬нioned when com┬нpared with the real world of the 1980s.тАЭ
Orwell does┬нnтАЩt even both┬нer to imag┬нine any new vices: тАЬHis char┬нac┬нters are all gin hounds and tobac┬нco addicts,тАЭ Asi┬нmov writes, тАЬand part of the hor┬нror of his pic┬нture of 1984 is his elo┬нquent descrip┬нtion of the low qual┬нi┬нty of the gin and tobac┬нco.тАЭ That telling detail hints at one of OrwellтАЩs major sources of inspi┬нra┬нtion: the British Min┬нistry of Infor┬нma┬нtion, his wifeтАЩs employ┬нer dur┬нing World War II, and the source of the mate┬нr┬нi┬нal he broad┬нcast to India while work┬нing at the BBC around the same time.┬а The Min┬нistryтАЩs can┬нteen, accord┬нing to his let┬нters, was not of the high┬нest stan┬нdard. WhatтАЩs more, the 850-word тАЬBasic Eng┬нlishтАЭ that it insist┬нed on using in its broad┬нcasts bears more than a pass┬нing resem┬нblance to Nine┬нteen Eight-FourтАЩs Newspeak, the pared-down lan┬нguage devel┬нoped and man┬нdat┬нed by the gov┬нern┬нment in order to lim┬нit its cit┬нi┬нzensтАЩ range of thought.
Asi┬нmov does┬нnтАЩt buy that either. тАЬThere is no sign that such com┬нpres┬нsions of the lan┬нguage have ever weak┬нened it as a mode of expres┬нsion,тАЭ he writes. тАЬAs a mat┬нter of fact, polit┬нi┬нcal obfus┬нca┬нtion has tend┬нed to use many words rather than few, long words rather than short, to extend rather than to reduce.тАЭ (This, of course, was some┬нthing Orwell knew.) What┬нev┬нer Nine┬нteen Eighty-FourтАЩs short┬нcom┬нings as prophe┬нcy, sci-fi, or indeed lit┬нer┬нa┬нture, Asi┬нmov does cred┬нit Orwell with a cer┬нtain geopo┬нlit┬нi┬нcal savvy. Its world-rul┬нing trio of Ocea┬нnia, Eura┬нsia, and Eas┬нt┬нa┬нsia тАЬfits in, very rough┬нly, with the three actu┬нal super┬нpow┬нers of the 1980s: the Unit┬нed States, the Sovi┬нet Union, and Chi┬нna.тАЭ Orwell knew, as many did┬нnтАЩt, that the lat┬нter two would not join forces, per┬нhaps thanks to his own frus┬нtrat┬нing expe┬нri┬нence fight┬нing for fac┬нtion┬нal┬нism-prone left caus┬нes. But not even as future-ori┬нent┬нed a mind as Asi┬нmovтАЩs would have guessed that, just a few years lat┬нer, the USSR would be out of the game тАФ and a few decades lat┬нer, the word Orwellian┬аwould be applied most often to Chi┬нna.
Read Asi┬нmovтАЩs take on 1984 here.
If you would like to sup┬нport the mis┬нsion of Open Cul┬нture, con┬нsid┬нer mak┬нing┬аa dona┬нtion to our site. ItтАЩs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬нtri┬нbu┬нtions will help us con┬нtin┬нue pro┬нvid┬нing the best free cul┬нtur┬нal and edu┬нca┬нtion┬нal mate┬нri┬нals to learn┬нers every┬нwhere. You can con┬нtribute through Pay┬нPal, Patre┬нon, and Ven┬нmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬нed con┬нtent:
An Ani┬нmat┬нed Intro┬нduc┬нtion to George Orwell
An Intro┬нduc┬нtion to George OrwellтАЩs 1984 and How Pow┬нer Man┬нu┬нfac┬нtures Truth
George Orwell Explains in a Reveal┬нing 1944 Let┬нter Why HeтАЩd Write 1984

Isaac Asi┬нmov Pre┬нdicts in 1964 What the World Will Look Like in 2014
Rid┬нley Scott on the Mak┬нing of AppleтАЩs Icon┬нic тАЬ1984тАЭ Com┬нmer┬нcial, Aired on Super Bowl Sun┬нday in 1984
Based in Seoul,┬аCol┬нin┬аMarshall┬аwrites and broad┬нcasts on cities, lan┬нguage, and cul┬нture. HeтАЩs the author of the newslet┬нter┬аBooks on Cities┬аas well as the books┬аэХЬъ╡н ьЪФьХ╜ ъ╕ИьзА┬а(No Sum┬нma┬нriz┬нing Korea) and┬аKore┬нan Newtro.┬аFol┬нlow him on the social net┬нwork for┬нmer┬нly known as Twit┬нter at┬а@colinmarshall.

title: Cinga Samson Conjures Mystery and the Sublime in Large-Scale Oil Paintings
description: Amid groves of trees, meadows, and aging infrastructure, Cinga SamsonтАЩs dreamlike tableaux are bathed in eerie light, as if spotlit or illuminated
body:
Amid groves of trees, meadows, and aging infrastructure, Cinga SamsonтАЩs dreamlike tableaux are bathed in eerie light, as if spotlit or illuminated by the moon. The South African artist is known for his use of deep, dark pigments such as carbon black and Prussian blue, complemented by the occasional teal or purple and pops of bright white in t-shirts or sneakers. His figures, engaged in enigmatic activities, look on with spectral, all-white eyes. Green and brown foliage camouflages individuals who gather in fields, sort through mysterious items, and appear to converge with other beings like large birds.
The work seen here is currently on view in the artistтАЩs solo exhibition at White Cube called Ukuphuthelwa. In the artistтАЩs native language, isiXhosa, the title means тАЬunable to sleep.тАЭ тАЬUnlike the English word тАШinsomnia,тАЩ the isiXhosa term carries no negative connotation, and accordingly, for Samson, sleeplessness is not a condition to be cured but a state of spiritual alertness, a sensitivity that deepens in the dark,тАЭ the gallery says. 
Detail of тАЬUkuwelwa komdaтАЭ
The nature of consciousness and even the realm of dreams is an apt entry point to SamsonтАЩs latest work, which examines how painting can be what he describes as тАЬtrue and honest.тАЭ What is real? What do we imagine? Throughout art history, animals have long symbolized certain qualities or beliefs, such as dogs as allegories of fidelity and birds as messengers between the earthly and the divine. In the artistтАЩs large-scale compositions, there is a sense of тАЬreadabilityтАЭ akin to the narrative-driven Western art genre of history paintings, where the presence of these beings hints at meaning. Yet as we spend time with SamsonтАЩs works curiosity and wonder seem to always overtake glimpses of recognition.
Even though the artistтАЩs scenes may seem at first indecipherable, there is a foundational sense of reverence and ceremony in how his figures commune in seemingly out-of-the-way places for undisclosed, perhaps ritualistic purposes. Samson invites us to encounter the unknown, weaving a tension between what is knowable and what is unspecified or concealed. Through this, he explores the limits of representation in art and aims for what the gallery describes as тАЬthe authority of the unnameable and the territory of the sublime,тАЭ where sacredness is present in holy spaces and everyday experiences alike.
Ukuphuthelwa continues through April 18 in New York City. See more on the artistтАЩs Instagram.
тАЬIyafunwaтАЭ (2026), oil on canvas, 86 1/2 x 103 3/8 inches
Detail of тАЬIyafunwaтАЭ
тАЬUmlindoтАЭ (2026), oil on canvas, 91 3/4 x 138 inches
Detail of тАЬUmlindoтАЭ
тАЬImfihloтАЭ (2026), oil on canvas, 55 x 110 1/2 inches
Detail тАЬImfihloтАЭ
тАЬIsiganekoтАЭ (2026), oil on canvas, 86 1/2 x 102 1/2 inches
Detail of тАЬIsiganekoтАЭ

title: Trump Plays Christ and Gets the Wound Wrong
description:
Opinion
                The presidentтАЩs latest attempt to fashion himself as an American messiah is costing him some of his most
body:
Opinion
                The presidentтАЩs latest attempt to fashion himself as an American messiah is costing him some of his most loyal Catholic supporters.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Trump's now-deleted Truth Social post contained an AI-generated image of him as Jesus (screenshot Hyperallergic)Yesterday, on Orthodox Easter, Donald Trump faced an eruption of backlash for posting an AI-generated photo of himself as Jesus Christ, just a couple of minutes after he criticized Pope Leo XIV on Truth Social. The image parodies the centuries-old Catholic image of the Divine Physician, with Trump wearing a flowing white mantle with a red robe, a beam of light emanating from his left hand as he rests his right on the forehead of a man in a hospital bed. Surrounding him are an older man in a dark navy cap (presumably a veteran), a man in military camouflage, a woman in a blue surgeon cap with a stethoscope, and a woman with hands folded in prayer тАФ all of whom are White. Behind him waves a rippling American flag, while eagles and fighter jets soar through the sky above the Statue of Liberty and the Lincoln Memorial.Trump posted the image right after going on a political tirade against Pope Leo about an hour after 60 Minutes aired a segment about the pope and American Catholic leadersтАЩ ongoing opposition against the presidentтАЩs war in Iran and immigration policies. In his post, Trump claims, тАЬIf I wasnтАЩt in the White House, Leo wouldnтАЩt be in the Vatican.тАЭThe post was deleted after outcry from his loyalist supporters, including Bishop Robert Barron, who serves on the presidential administrationтАЩs Religious Liberty Commission, formed last May. Conservative pundit Michael Knowles тАФ himself a practicing Catholic тАФ posted that тАЬit behooves the President both spiritually and politically to delete the picture, no matter the intent.тАЭ Even the Knights Templar International, a far-right Catholic group that has long supported Trump, said they тАЬdemand that this offensive and blasphemous image be removed forthwith.тАЭ Today, Trump denied that the AI image depicted him as Jesus, claiming that it rendered him instead as a doctor.Last May, I wrote about how Trump posted an AI-generated meme of himself as the pope as a way of virtue signaling to MAGA Catholics. However, his latest attempt to fashion himself as an American messiah is costing him some of his most loyal Catholic supporters, who are now faced with the choice of publicly supporting the pope or the president. ItтАЩs not the first time that Catholics have had to choose.Despite the recent ascendency of wealthy White Catholics into positions of power, anti-Catholic sentiment is deeply rooted in American history. For much of the nationтАЩs lifespan, Catholics were targeted and discriminated against due to anti-Catholic, xenophobic bigotry. In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, as Catholic immigrants from Southern and Eastern Europe arrived in the United States, they faced intense nativist violence, including from the Ku Klux Klan. Now, the Trump administration is wielding nativist violence against immigrants and communities of color тАФ including Catholic Latinos тАФ whom he accuses of taking resources away from тАЬtrueтАЭ Americans: White Protestants.TrumpтАЩs absurdist image of himself as Jesus with a fake tan makes clear who he has come to тАЬsave.тАЭ Just like 19th- and 20th-century anti-Catholic campaigns, Trump fashions himself a Messiah for American Protestant Christian nationalists, whom he blesses with a blazing, white light emanating from his left hand.But art historians and cradle Catholics alike will recognize that light is emanating out of the wrong body part тАФ light poured out of JesusтАЩs side wound, not his hand. ItтАЩs a perfect illustration of just how little Trump knows about the art and religion he is weaponizing, all in the name of his false White Christian Jesus.
                  
                      
                        Opinion
                      
                      
                        Donald Trump

schema.headline: Trump Plays Christ and Gets the Wound Wrong

title: Gagosian to Debut New Gallery With DuchampтАЩs тАЬReadymadesтАЭ
description:
News
                The exhibition will run concurrently with the artist's first United States retrospective in over 50 years a
body:
News
                The exhibition will run concurrently with the artist's first United States retrospective in over 50 years at the Museum of Modern Art.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Marcel Duchamp inside the exhibition The Art of Assemblage at the Museum of Modern Art, New York, 1961, with his artworks тАЬFountainтАЭ (1950, replica of lost 1917 original) and тАЬBicycle WheelтАЭ (1951, replica of lost 1913 original) (┬й Association Marcel Duchamp/ADAGP, Paris/ Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York 2026; courtesy Gagosian, photo Marvin Lazarus)Remember brat summer? Well, apparently New York City is determined to have a Duchamp spring as Gagosian gallery announced that the inaugural exhibition at its new Uptown location will center the conceptual artist's famous тАЬreadymadesтАЭ тАФ including a replica series first exhibited at the same location over six decades ago. ┬аOpening April 25 and running concurrently with the artist's first United States retrospective in over 50 years at the Museum of Modern Art, Gagosian's forthcoming exhibition will present several of Duchamp's most recognized readymades тАФ pre-existing, mass-produced objects that the artist designated as artworks.The show is a tale of new beginnings at the same place for both the mega-gallery and the artist. Having opened its first NYC location at 980 Madison Avenue in 1989, Gagosian was recently forced to vacate its multi-level headquarters after Bloomberg Philanthropies bought out the building in 2024. Nevertheless, art dealer Larry Gagosian was determined to stay in the NYC flagship location of 35 years, and managed to secure a ground-level space at 980 Madison Avenue for the gallery.Installation view of Marcel Duchamp at the Museum of Modern Art (photo Lisa Yin Zhang/Hyperallergic)In 1964, Duchamp and his dealer, Arturo Schwarz, commemorated the 50th anniversary of the artist's first readymade тАФ тАЬBicycle WheelтАЭ (1913) тАФ by creating a limited edition set of 14 authorized replicas of original works that had been lost over time. The replica set made its American debut in a 1965 exhibition at Cordier & Ekstrom Gallery, then housed in the same Upper East Side building.(As it turns out, Cordier & Ekstrom Gallery also lost its lease at 980 Madison Avenue after 20 years in 1979 ... Time is a flat circle тАФ nay, a spinning bike wheel.)Since their inception, Duchamp's readymades have maintained a polarizing influence on modern and contemporary art, provoking conversations about what the term тАЬartтАЭ encompasses, and who gets to determine that, for over a century. It was тАЬFountainтАЭ (1917), an overturned porcelain urinal that the artist signed off with the pseudonym R. Mutt, that catapulted the body of work into fiery debate after Duchamp submitted it for display in an inaugural un-juried exhibition hosted by the Society of Independent Artists in NYC.Marcel Duchamp, тАЬL.H.O.O.Q.тАЭ (1964, after the 1919 original) (┬й Association Marcel Duchamp/ADAGP, Paris/ Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York 2026; courtesy Gagosian, photo Owen Conwa)The work's ultimate exclusion from the main show, which aimed to display all artists' submissions so long as they paid the required fee, led to Duchamp's resignation from the Society. The dialogue about the work's value and artistic designation continued in the following issue of the Blind Man, a short-lived Dadaist journal, which included a segment in defense of тАЬFountain.тАЭAlongside replicas of тАЬFountainтАЭ and тАЭBicycle Wheel,тАЭ GagosianтАЩs forthcoming exhibition will also include Duchamp's тАЬL.H.O.O.Q.тАЭ (1964, after 1919 original); тАЬPorte-bouteilles (Bottle Dryer)тАЭ (1964, after 1914 lost original); and тАЬBo├оte-en-valiseтАЭ (1935тАУ49; contents 1935тАУ41).┬аA spokesperson for the gallery told Hyperallergic that additional details regarding the included artworks and the format of the new gallery space would become available closer to the exhibitionтАЩs opening date. 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        New York
                      
                      
                        Marcel Duchamp
                      
                      
                        Gagosian Gallery

title: Ai Weiwei and the Art of Keeping Your Mouth Shut
description:
Book Review
                тАЬOn CensorshipтАЭ offers timely reflections from the dissident artist, whose entire life and career ha
body:
Book Review
                тАЬOn CensorshipтАЭ offers timely reflections from the dissident artist, whose entire life and career have been marked by state persecution.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Cover of On Censorship by Ai Weiwei (Thames & Hudson, 2026)ItтАЩs hard to maintain dignity and self-respect when your mouth is gagged. You keep your head low, lips tight, and social media feed squeaky clean. You distrust friends and colleagues you used to run your mouth to. You feel temporary relief when itтАЩs not you whoтАЩs snatched from bed in the middle of the night and sent to a remote detention camp. But you know youтАЩre living on borrowed time: How long can you silence the very thing that makes you human?┬аCensorship, writes dissident Chinese artist Ai Weiwei in a small but mighty new book, тАЬstrips innocence from the young and kindness from the elderly. It discourages people from valuing justice, fostering selfishness instead.тАЭ┬а┬аOn Censorship (2026) packs in ever-timely reflections from an artist whose entire life and career have been marked by state persecution. As a child, he saw his father, poet and activist Ai Qing, clean communal toilets in a labor camp. As an adult, his exhibitions were axed, his reputation was tarnished, and his studio was raided and surveilled. And when that wasnтАЩt enough to silence him, the ruling Communist Party detained him for 81 days without charge in 2011. He was later condemned to a life of exile. More recently, authorities even made sure the Chinese AI chatbot DeepSeek would forget him.┬а┬аIn the early days of IsraelтАЩs genocide in Gaza, Ai learned the hard way that sheltering in the West had its limitations, too. In November 2023, Lisson Gallery canceled his show at its London location after he posted his thoughts about the conflict on social media. For a moment, one of the worldтАЩs biggest artists became as powerless as the university students being penalized for protesting for Palestinian rights.тАЬIn the western world, censorship alone cannot achieve its goals; it relies heavily on self-censorship,тАЭ he explains, reminding people in self-styled democracies that theyтАЩre not safe, either.┬аSelf-censorship, this silent killer, is perhaps the most soul-eroding part of life under an authoritarian regime. In a distorted, upside-down world of fear and inhibition, war is peace, submission is freedom, and ignorance is strength.┬аWe end up with a society that is тАЬnumb, devoid of emotional depth, perception and creativity,тАЭ and that is тАЬmarked by self-mockery and self-consolation,тАЭ warns Ai.┬аWhat else could we call the recent tragic example of filmmaker Wim Wenders, who twisted himself into pretzels while trying to answer a question about the Berlin International Film Festival's silence on Gaza? You could see the shock and misery, almost grief, on his face when he realized that the words тАЬwe have to stay out of politicsтАЭ had just left his mouth.┬аTo be fair to Wenders, how many of us have pursed our lips at the bitter aftertaste of biting our tongues and swallowing our words over the past two and a half years? How many of us have played dumb to keep out of trouble?┬а┬а┬аThe most useful part of On Censorship is a reminder that the power to reclaim freedom of speech is in our hands. Censorship and surveillance only work if we participate in them. The moment we stop fearing it, the system will collapse all at once.On Censorship (2026) by Ai Weiwei is published by Thames & Hudson and is available online and through independent booksellers.
                  
                      
                        Book Review
                      
                      
                        Books
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        Ai Weiwei

schema.headline: Ai Weiwei and the Art of Keeping Your Mouth Shut

title: Chicana Painter Criselda Vasquez Says ICE Detained Her Father
description:
News
                Vasquez was inspired by her Mexican immigrant parents to paint her celebrated 2017 portrait тАЬThe New Americ
body:
News
                Vasquez was inspired by her Mexican immigrant parents to paint her celebrated 2017 portrait тАЬThe New American Gothic.тАЭ
            
                
                                
                            
                            
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Criselda Vasquez's parents posing in front of the artist's painting тАЬThe New American GothicтАЭ (2017) (photo courtesy the artist)Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has detained the father and portrait muse of California-based Chicana painter Criselda Vasquez, the artist said in an Instagram post. тАЬMy father and one of his workers were detained by ICE while simply on their way to work,тАЭ Vasquez wrote on April 3. тАЬOur entire family is heartbroken, and my mother is completely devastated.тАЭ The family has not publicly named Vasquez's father. Vasquez noted in her post that he had been in the United States for over 40 years now.In an April 13 email to Hyperallergic, Vasquez said that her father had been тАЬracially profiled on his way home from work, and┬аtaken by Immigration authorities,тАЭ on Tuesday, March 31, adding that he had been pulled over and arrested right outside of a neighbor's home.тАЬMy family and I have since made contact with him and know which detention facility he is being held in,тАЭ Vasquez said. Through a GoFundMe linked on the artist's social media, the family raised nearly $68,000 of a $80,000 goal within 10 days of its launch. In a statement attached to the fundraiser, the family said the money would go toward his lost wages and legal fees associated with his detention.тАЬWhat I want the public to know about my father is that he is a devoted husband, parent, and grandfather, and the hardest-working, most selfless person I know,тАЭ Vasquez continued in her email to Hyperallergic. тАЬHe has become a role model not only to his four children, all of whom are United States citizens, but also to many people he has come across.тАЭтАЬMy father's hard work, dedication, and sacrifices will never be forgotten and will continue to be a long lifetime inspiration to me and my family,тАЭ Vasquez said.Criselda Vasquez, тАЬThe New American GothicтАЭ (2017) (Copyright ┬й 2017 Criselda Vasquez; All rights reserved)Vasquez depicted her father in her celebrated 2017 painting тАЬThe New American GothicтАЭ (2017), a reimagining of Grant Wood's тАЬAmerican GothicтАЭ (1930) featuring both of her parents, originally from Mexico, holding cleaning products and a hoe. The Lucas Museum for Narrative Art in Los Angeles acquired the painting in 2021. In an earlier statement about the work, the artist had said that she sought to depict the тАЬplight and the plight of many in my community.тАЭIn a previous social media post, Vasquez described her parents' influence on her figurative painting practice, which seeks to unveil hidden aspects of the Mexican-American life. тАЬWhen my parents pose for these paintings, their faces are reduced to extremely raw and somehow vulnerable expressions,тАЭ Vasquez said. тАЬSadly, they strive to be invisible every day. They have dealt with constant rejection, suspicion, and fear so long, that it seems now that it comes naturally to them.тАЭтАЬIn the long tradition of immigrants that come to the United States, they have made homes here, and they are just trying to live a simple life with a bit of security and hopefulness for their children,тАЭ Vasquez continued.The news of Vasquez's father's arrest comes amid President Trump's ongoing crackdown on immigrants in the United States. A recent report from the Deportation Data Project, which analyzes immigration enforcement data, outlined that ICE street arrests тАФ as in those occurring in neighborhoods, immigration courts, and check-in appointments at ICE offices тАФ had increased more than elevenfold during the first year of Trump's second presidency. It's also significantly harder to get released from immigration detention amid deportation proceedings, the report notes. 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE)
                      
                      
                        Criselda Vasquez
                      
                      
                        California
                      
                      
                        Immigration
                      
                      
                        Trump Administration
